speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape number six of the interview with Richard Pick in Mexico City, Mexico. Could you talk about how you met up with family, or how you find out what happened to your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I actually find out about my family when I saw one of my family members. I don't want to jump ahead. So we were in the Seventh Army Interrogation Center. It was the-- now it was VJ Day. VE Day was victory in Europe. And VJ day was victory in Japan-- which I think it was in September of 1945. And I went to Heidelberg on a-- on a Sunday. And Monday morning at 6 o'clock, I had to be back. It was an old garrison where that Seventh Army Interrogation Center was located at the time. And I was all wet because we were not allowed to put up the the--

speaker: interviewer
text: Hood?

speaker: interviewee
text: What do you call it, on the car? The, the canvas. So it was raining, and I was all wet when I got back. I had my identification, the card, And of course, the Puerto  Rican guards, they knew me. I went to the garrison, and went into my room. And we had our cots. And we were three, in that particular room. And the two were still asleep. And there was another guy sleeping on my cot, which got me quite upset because I figured that they must have had one too many, you know, celebrating VJ Day. Now the war really was over, for us because before we thought they might ship us to Japan, or to one of the islands, there, in the Pacific.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I was mad. I walked over to my cot. I pulled the guy. He turned around. It was my brother. I almost fainted. I almost fainted. I couldn't believe it. I saw it was my brother. He looked terrible. He looked terrible. He said, Richard, he said, are you married? That was his first question because, when he had arrived, he didn't speak English. And he told the Puerto Ricans that he had a brother there. But how did he get to Seckenheim? He had gone to Stutthof. He had come our of a concentration camp. He was working near Buckenwald because the last ones who still lived, they shipped to factories under SS guards to work now for the Germans, a couple of months before the war was over. So he was in Buckenwald. But he worked outside of Buckenwald. In fact, during the night he was being walked to the concentration camp, to Buckenwald. So when the war was over, he walked. He started to walk, with a friend, in the wrong direction. Even before the war was over, they took off because, now, the SS people, they closed one eye because they knew what was going to happen. So he took off with a friend.

speaker: interviewee
text: But they walked east-- they walked into the hands of the Russians. And the Russians put them into a camp with German prisoners of war. So they tried to make themselves understand, the two of them, that they were Jews. They were in the concentration camp. They should look at them.

speaker: interviewee
text: So they didn't speak Russian. And the Russians, of course, they were very, very primitive, at the time. The soldiers didn't even know how to-- how to read a watch. They had-- some of them had never seen a watch in their lives. It was incredible. So they called an officer, a Jewish officer, who spoke a little Jewish. And he told them, they are Jews. So he asked them for some German prayers, you know, the Shema, and things like that, which he recited. He showed him that he was circumcised, which at the time, meant a lot because nowadays almost everybody is circumcised. At the time, though, only Jews were circumcised. So after two or three days, they let them go. And they walked on horseback. After about three weeks, or four weeks, he got to Stutthof. There was no transportation at the time, you know.

speaker: interviewee
text: And when he got to Stutthof, he-- everything was rationed, you know, very little food. So he got coupons for the food. And even then, you had to stand in line to get whatever they had the coupons for. So he was standing in line, and all of a sudden, say one shouts, Lutz! I mean, his name, now, is Larry. He calls himself Larry. But at the time, his name was Lutz.

speaker: interviewee
text: Lutz! That was Alfred Liebster, the fellow I had seen in [NON-ENGLISH] a few months earlier. And Alfred said, did you see your brother? And Lutz said, my brother? He is in America. He said, no. He said, he was here a few months ago. I talked with him. He's in the US Army.

speaker: interviewee
text: He told him the whole story. He gave him the APO number. And my brother found out the APO number from the military government which was established in Stutthof at the time, where my unit was. And one of the officer says, I'm going to go to Heidelberg, and if you want me to, shortly, I'll take you along. That's how Larry got to Seckenheim. He actually walked from Heidelberg to Seckenheim, which is about a two or three hour's walk. When he got there, he-- that's now his story. The Puerto Ricans called one of the officers. The officers-- he, he interrogated him outside of the premises. And then he asked him a few questions about myself, when I was born, my birthday, et cetera, the name of our parents. He checked it against my file while I was still in Heidelberg. It was us on that Sunday. And he said, all right, come in. And he talked to the commanding officer. The commanding officer said, all right. Gave him to eat, yes, and but nothing else. Just give him to eat whatever he wants. And he has to stay in the room, and he can sleep in, in Richard's bed. And that's how I-- how I found him-- or he found me.

speaker: interviewee
text: And his first question was, are you married? I said, why do you ask this? He said, well, I opened your locker. We all had lockers-- these foot lockers. And there were a few pictures hanging there, you know. And they were pictures of two small children, which I had taken with a girl there in France someplace. And a few more pictures. And he thought that these could be my children. So, to make a long story short, there was a big celebration, of course. And he said listen-- I said, how about our parents, and the rest of the family? And he said, listen Richard, bad news. And I-- he started to cry. I cried.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I-- every, every word, practically, I had to press out of him. I had to ask, and ask, and ask. He said, all right, Richard. When I calm down, I will tell you the story once. But never ask me again. And this is actually what he did. He, he was there in the-- he was in different concentration camps, and ghettos, too. And in the ghetto of Riga, they took my, my father away with other people, what they called the kommando. But a kommando meant that that was the end.

speaker: interviewee
text: And when the guards came back from the kommando who had taken my father, and my brother-- they saw each other all day long, you know. And many of those guards were not Germans, but were, were Lithuanians-- Baltic people who volunteered for the German SS, you know? And some of those, or many of those, were [INAUDIBLE] that the German SS. They wanted to prove something, you know, to the Germans. So he, he, he had asked that guard what happened to my father. And he said, he was beaten to death. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] My mother was in Stutthof, where my brother was for about four weeks. My brother-- I'm going back now to '44. They had arrived in Stutthof from Riga in September '44. My brother was shipped out in October. My mother was still there in Stutthof.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I was always under the impression, talking to one or two survivors of Stutthof, that my mother-- Stutthof was actually a relatively small concentration camp. They only had one oven there, and one gas chamber.

speaker: interviewee
text: So when I talked to, to my brother about this, he said that, that my mother, when he saw her last, that she was in pretty bad shape. And that was in-- that was in September that she was in pretty bad shape. They hardly had anything to eat. And then when I talked to someone else who was survivor of Stutthof who was there until the very last. She said to me, that she thinks that my mother must have died at the beginning of December because she was practically starved to death.

speaker: interviewee
text: The rest of my family, I found out, not from, from people, but from records because a few years ago, my brother Larry, and my wife Laura, and my sister-in-law, we went to Poland on an organized trip. And we went also to Theresienstadt, to Czechoslovakia. We went to Stutthof. We took a car to Stutthof because the group didn't go to Stutthof. They were in Warsaw. And it was a Saturday-- it was a Shabbat. And the group was free to do whatever they wanted to do because some, maybe, wanted to go to synagogue. And we talked it over Friday night. I already said, let's take a car, let's rent a car, and let's go to Stutthof. From Warsaw to go to Stutthof is about, I would say, maybe three or three and a half hours, one way. Stutthof is at the Baltic. It's near what they call, today, Gdansk. At the time, it was called Danzig. It's also very close of where the-- where they-- where they tried on the life of Hitler in June of 1944-- just about 15 or 20 miles from there. The guide, in fact, asked us whether we wanted to stop by there. But frankly, we, we wanted to go to Stutthof. We went to Stuffhof, and we wanted to go-- come back again the same day because there were no hotels at the time. It was still communist, you know-- Poland. And we went to Stutthof. And it was, of course, a terrible sight. It's a museum now. It's-- they built it up again. They, they painted it. I mean, they, they did all kinds of maintenance work. While we were in Stutthof, we saw a group of people working with shovels from far away. We wondered what they were doing, and we walked over there. And we started to talk to a young lady, maybe 24 years old. She said she is working on her doctorate, on her dissertation, on modern history. And she selected Stutthof, the concentration camp, Stutthof.

speaker: interviewee
text: And she's here with a group of-- what do they call them-- well, Germans. These were East Germans. At the time, there was still East Germany, East Berlin. They were from East Berlin, from a university in East Berlin. And they were a group. They-- to do good again. What they call it? You know, I mean, to try to, to bring restitution to the Jews, you know? So they worked, and she told us they do maintenance work on their vacations, and she does research for her dissertation. So I asked, I mean, why do dig here, and what do you do with these measuring rods? So she says, come over. She showed us. They were going-- with iron rods, they were going down into the ground-- thin rods, going down into the ground-- and they were measuring it. And I say, what are you doing?

speaker: interviewee
text: She said, we are trying to measure how many people were cremated here. We are trying to measure the, the cubic feet of the ashes underground. And she said, we are also doing some digging. And she took us about 60 or 80 feet further under a group of trees to a big, heap pile of old shoes, full of dirt, and some of them were gone. I mean, the leather it was all molded.

speaker: interviewee
text: And she said, do you see the work we are doing? This is coming out, right now. You can-- you can watch it, while they're doing it. They were digging up the shoes?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were digging up to whatever they could. But most of it were shoes because most of whatever else there was, was gone. But the leather still, still was there.

speaker: interviewee
text: So and tears were coming to her eyes when she mentioned to us that she said what-- every so often, you know, she says, I get a hold of a baby shoe, she says. And this is terrible. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: From there, we went back to Warsaw. And from Warsaw, we went to Auschwitz. Laura, my wife, and my sister-in-law Lottie they said, we just cannot take it any longer. After Stutthof, they had had it. And Larry and I, we went to, to Auschwitz. Larry at first didn't want to go either. He said, listen, I don't think I can do it. And I said, listen, I want to see where the rest of my family most likely died-- you know, where they were killed. And we went to Auschwitz, and, of course, was terrible. I mean, I took two carousel, 144 slides each, of, of slides. Photography was one of my hobbies. It still is. And I have those trays, and anyone who wants to see them, they are welcome. Of the whole trip-- it's not only Auschwitz and Stutthof. There are other sites, too. And then Auschwitz, of course, was a-- in relation to, to Stutthof-- Stutthof was, how shall I say-- it was, was very small. It was-- it was a good size concentration camp, but in relation to Auschwitz, Auschwitz was a death factory. In Auschwitz, I haven't counted them, but they must have had, easily, I would say, eight or ten ovens. They had different gas chambers.

speaker: interviewee
text: They had-- just for the museum's sake, they had one room just full of shoes. I mean, they-- probably they had many more. Toothbrushes, and shaving-- at the time, men shaved with shaving cream, with soap, and shaving brushes. The shaving brushes, they had there. Old razors, they had in there. Suitcases, they had in there when the people arrived there from the trains, you know? They had the silvers, the, the names of the people. You know, the people put their names on their suitcases? They were supposed to do that before the were deported.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I remember that. That was a very placed-- very-- in that the showpiece there behindd-- it was all behind, behind a glass. It said, kleinkind, so and so. Kleinkind means infant, so and so.

speaker: interviewee
text: So this was mighty sad, a terrible experience and still inspired. I must say, if I wouldn't have done it, I think I would do it because I think every Jew has to do it. And especially, not the old ones, like we-- but I think the young ones should go there. Every young Jew should go there.

speaker: interviewee
text: And it's not in bad taste. And I think it would also do good if Gentiles and Christians would go there too to see what, what was done by, by the master race, by people that at the time, were supposed to be culturally-- not only militarily. That, everyone knew-- but culturally, the most advanced people in this world. I mean, let's face it-- so many famous poets, sculptors, physicians, physicists, writers, authors, actors, musicians, you name it, came from that milieu. And to get back of it, as a German Jew, I must say, while the German population-- while the Jewish population in Germany was less than 1% of the total population of Germany, the German Jews participated 30% as far as Nobel Prize winners go-- thirty times. And those people are killed. Very famous authors, musicians, painters, writers, were killed just like anyone else.

speaker: interviewer
text: It's been more than 50 years now since the end of the war, and you've come out to tell your story. Is there anything that you would like us, as listeners, to learn, to know, to remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. To remember-- well, to remember, I mean we all know what happened. But, I would say, to the news media and to the politicians especially, and to many people, I would say, [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] stop talking and start acting. Start doing something about it. We are only fooling ourselves. Shalom, peace, never again-- these are all slogans. When I was drafted, and when I was in basic training in the US Army, we were told it was going to be the war to end all wars. Never again. Look at what used to be Yugoslavia. And never again-- I'm afraid, it could happen again. We all very shortsighted. We think that we are-- we have arrived at the top. I'm talking about the Jews now because they are doing quite well nowadays economically, socially. I mean, they are peers. They have become peers. They have become peers, especially since the state of Israel has been created. Before the state of Israel was created, we were not so much of peers. Now there is a backing. We have a land. We have something to fall back on, even though many Jews don't realize this. But since that particular moment, I think that even though we don't realize it, we have become more, more even with the rest of the population. They are not looking down on us-- at least not so much any more as they used to. And I think Israel has a lot to do with this. God forbid, if Israel wouldn't exist, I don't know what would happen again.

speaker: interviewer
text: And even in the United States, I feel-- maybe because I have-- with the experiences I have-- I think that, sooner or later, the United States will have to go way to the right. Why? Because, look at criminality. Look at pollution. Look at the poverty. Look at welfare. We have not advanced very much, I would say, as a country. So democracy-- as I said before, I'm a US citizen. I'm very proud to be a US citizen. I am very grateful to the United States. I would do anything for the United States. But through democracy, obviously, we have not been able to solve these problems. There are just too many discussions, too many committees which are being formed where they don't have the answers for something. And then, all of a sudden after [INAUDIBLE] died out, and you don't hear about it anymore. So I feel that somehow, to [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] win the war against criminality, against the pollution-- because there's a lot of graft going on, too, you know-- I am afraid that, to find a solution, the United States will have to move a little bit away from that free speech. Look at all the pornography. They, they, they cover themselves, and they get away with it with the-- with the Constitution. They refer to the free speech and liberty. But what is being done under this-- under this [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] name? What negative things are being done, day in and day out, using free speech as a cover? So I think that, sooner or later, things will move to the right. And Buchanan will not make it this year. But someone like Buchanan, I'm afraid, sooner or later, will make it. Maybe it will not be as pronounced at first. But something will have to give because we have not been able to, to solve these problems.
